planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; crown flower is one of Hawaii's most beloved lei flowers — its large, waxy, white or purple flowers are the traditional choice for royal leis — and is sacred to Lord Shiva in Hindu tradition; very drought-tolerant once established; ALL plant parts except the outer petals are toxic — wear gloves when pruning and keep away from children.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; crown flower is extremely drought-tolerant and thrives on neglect; CAUTION: All parts except outer petals are toxic — wear gloves when handling. Calotropis gigantea (crown flower, giant milkweed, or mudar) is a large tropical shrub to small tree native to South Asia and Southeast Asia — one of the most important ornamental, cultural, and medicinal plants across the tropics. The plant produces large, thick, ovate, silver-blue-green leaves coated with a waxy, white powder, and spectacular clusters of waxy, star-shaped flowers with a distinctive crown-shaped secondary corona — each individual flower resembling a tiny crown, in white or lavender-purple. In Hawaii, crown flower (particularly the white form) has been the classic choice for making long, fragrant leis for over a century; Queen Lili'uokalani was famous for wearing long white crown flower leis and the plant is deeply associated with Hawaiian royalty. In Hindu tradition across South Asia, the flowers are sacred offerings to Lord Shiva and are used extensively in religious ceremonies and temple garlands. The milky white sap (latex) is toxic and irritating; the bark fiber has traditional uses in rope-making. A magnificent, bold-textured, drought-tolerant ornamental for zone 9–12 that combines ornamental beauty with deep cultural significance.
sources: University of Hawaii at Manoa College of Tropical Agriculture — Lei Plants
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calotropis species
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

common_name: Mysore Trumpetvine (Lady's Slipper Vine, Dolls Shoes)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Mysore trumpetvine is one of the most spectacular flowering vines for zone 9–12 — the long, pendulous clusters of exotic, red-and-yellow bicolor tubular flowers (looking like a bunch of hanging torches or dancing dolls' shoes) are among the most visually stunning in the tropical plant world, attracting hummingbirds and sunbirds from a distance.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; provide a very strong pergola or trellis — this vine grows vigorously and becomes very heavy; prune after flowering. Thunbergia mysorensis (Mysore trumpetvine, lady's slipper vine, or dolls shoes vine) is a spectacular evergreen climbing vine from the subtropical montane forests of southern India — particularly the Mysore region of Karnataka (where the common name originates) — and one of the most dramatically beautiful flowering vines in tropical horticulture. The vine produces long (12–18 inch), pendulous, racemose clusters of exotic, bicolor tubular flowers: deep red to burgundy-red on the outside with yellow-cream interiors and reflexed lobes, hanging in dense, eye-catching chains that resemble a row of dancing dolls or hanging slippers — hence the evocative common names. The flower clusters drape elegantly over pergolas, fences, and arbors, creating one of the most visually arresting and tropical-looking displays possible in a garden. The flowers are produced abundantly for months in warm climates and attract hummingbirds, bees, and sunbirds. The vine grows vigorously to 20–30 feet on strong support structures. Thunbergia mysorensis is becoming increasingly available through specialty tropical nurseries as awareness of its spectacular ornamental value spreads beyond its native range.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Thunbergia species
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Specimen Vines

common_name: Cup of Gold Vine (Golden Chalice Vine, Hawaiin Lily)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; cup of gold vine is one of the most spectacular large-flowered vines for zone 9–12 — the enormous (6–9 inch), golden-yellow, chalice-shaped flowers with five dark-purple stripes in the throat open in late winter and spring, filling the air with a rich, vanilla-coconut fragrance; ALL parts of the plant are toxic; provide very sturdy structural support.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; provide extremely strong support — this vine becomes enormously heavy; CAUTION: All parts are toxic. Solandra maxima (cup of gold vine or golden chalice vine) is a rampant, large-leafed tropical climbing shrub from Mexico, Central America, and the Caribbean — a member of the nightshade family (Solanaceae) and one of the most impressively large-flowered ornamental vines available for zone 9–12 gardens. The flowers are exceptional: enormous (6–9 inches across), goblet or chalice-shaped, with five creamy-yellow to golden petals fused into a spectacular cup form, each petal marked with a deep-purple to chocolate-brown stripe running from the center to the tip — an elegant and unusual color combination. The flowers have a powerful, sweet, vanilla-coconut fragrance that perfumes the garden, especially in the evenings. The main bloom period is late winter through spring, when the heavy vine covers itself in the golden chalices. The thick, glossy, leathery leaves are bold and attractive when not in flower. Solandra requires an extremely strong support structure (heavy wooden pergola or steel framework) as established plants can reach 40+ feet and become enormously heavy and woody. CAUTION: All parts of the plant are poisonous; the plants produce tropane alkaloids similar to Datura and Brugmansia.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Solandra maxima
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tropical Vines

common_name: Cardinal Creeper (Red Morning Glory, Hybrid Cypress Vine)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in full sun after last frost in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil; cardinal creeper is a vigorous annual hybrid vine (cross between Ipomoea quamoclit and I. coccinea) combining the finely divided, feathery foliage of cypress vine with slightly larger, cardinal-red, star-shaped flowers; extremely fast-growing, blooms summer through frost, and attracts hummingbirds prolifically.

directions: Direct sow in lean, well-drained soil in full sun after soil warms; nick or soak seeds 24 hours before planting; the vine grows quickly to 6–15 feet with minimal care. Ipomoea x multifida (cardinal creeper or cardinal vine) is a naturally occurring hybrid between cypress vine (I. quamoclit) and red morning glory (I. coccinea) — combining the most appealing ornamental features of both parents. From cypress vine, it inherits the deeply pinnately cut, feathery, bright-green foliage that creates an extraordinarily delicate, lacy texture. From red morning glory, it inherits slightly larger, more robust flowers (3/4–1 inch across) of the same intense cardinal-red as its parents. The result is a vigorous, fast-growing, annual ornamental vine that covers trellises, fences, and arbors with a curtain of delicate, feathery foliage and an abundance of small, brilliant-red, star-shaped tubular flowers from summer through fall. The flowers are irresistible to hummingbirds, which visit constantly throughout the day. The vine self-sows readily in warm climates and can be treated as a perennial in zone 9–10. A classic hummingbird garden plant that requires almost no care once established on a trellis.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ipomoea species
USDA PLANTS Database — Ipomoea x multifida

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in full sun after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; American basil (Ocimum americanum) is a distinct basil species from Africa and Asia — despite the misleading common name — with a flavor profile somewhat different from sweet basil (O. basilicum), featuring lighter, more lemony-herbal, slightly more resinous notes; used in West African, East African, and South Asian cooking; more drought-tolerant and heat-resistant than sweet basil.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after soil warms in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; pinch regularly to prevent bolting and encourage bushy growth; more heat and drought tolerant than sweet basil. Ocimum americanum (American basil, spice basil, or hoary basil) is a distinct basil species with a somewhat confusing common name — it originated in Africa and Asia, not the Americas, though it has become naturalized in tropical regions worldwide including the Americas. It differs from sweet basil (O. basilicum) in several ways: the leaves are smaller and lighter green, often with a slightly hairy (hoary) surface texture; the flavor is lighter, more lemony-citrus, with less of the clove-richness of sweet basil and a more herbal, slightly resinous character. O. americanum is widely used in West African cooking (particularly in Nigeria, Ghana, and Cameroon, where it is known as 'efirin' and is a common soup herb), East African cuisine, and in parts of South and Southeast Asia where it grows wild. It is significantly more drought-tolerant and heat-resistant than cultivated sweet basil, making it a good choice for hot, dry gardens where O. basilicum struggles. Several cultivated basil varieties are hybrids between O. americanum and O. basilicum, including lemon basil cultivars. The plant produces attractive white flower spikes and is a good pollinator plant.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Ocimum species
Nigerian Journal of Botany — Ocimum americanum Ethnobotany
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

common_name: Golden Lemon Balm (Gold Variegated Lemon Balm)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; golden lemon balm ('Aurea') differs meaningfully from the species (M. officinalis) primarily in its sun requirements — the golden-yellow variegated leaves burn and scorch in full sun, making this the best lemon balm cultivar for shady herb gardens, and the striking gold-and-green leaf coloring makes it an outstanding ornamental as well as a culinary herb; propagate by division (seed does not come true).

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; golden lemon balm requires shade protection — full sun bleaches and scorches the golden leaves; divide clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Melissa officinalis 'Aurea' (golden lemon balm) is a variegated cultivar of common lemon balm distinguished by its striking foliage: bright golden-yellow to gold-spashed-green leaves that glow in dappled shade conditions, creating a luminous effect in the shaded herb garden. Unlike the green-leafed species, which tolerates full sun well, 'Aurea' requires partial to dappled shade — the golden pigmentation means it lacks the green chlorophyll needed to handle direct sun exposure, causing the leaves to scorch and bleach in bright direct sunlight. This makes 'Aurea' uniquely valuable as the best lemon balm choice for shaded herb gardens, woodland herb borders, and partially shaded areas where the standard green form becomes stressed. The culinary properties and lemon-mint flavor of the golden form are identical to the green species — the fresh or dried leaves make the same pleasant lemon-herbal tea, the same addition to salads and garnishes, and the same aromatic edging plant. The golden foliage makes 'Aurea' outstanding for purely ornamental use in shade gardens even when not harvested for culinary purposes. Does not come true from seed — must be propagated from divisions of the parent plant.
sources: Herb Society of America — Lemon Balm (Melissa officinalis)
University of California Cooperative Extension — Shade Herbs
